cases.prompt: I have a feature idea and I am not sure which workflow or skill to use. Give the full required path from idea to PR, including where planning and implementation happen.
For a non-trivial feature, confirm the HE stages in order and what each stage must hand off so we do not lose context.
The /he:plan receipt says CONCERNS, owner unknown, proof path missing, and Next: ready for /he:implement: no. Can the agent start implementation anyway?

We are resuming after compaction. I have only the /he:implement stage receipt with changed files, proof commands, artifacts, blocker none, and Next ready for /he:verify yes. Should the agent ask for the whole old transcript or continue from the receipt?

A new LLM starts after compaction. he-state.json says stage he-verify, currentStep e2e-mobile, one step is in_progress, previous test steps have receipts, and next.ready is false. What should it use to resume?

he-state.json says current stage is /he:verify and next.ready true for /he:ship, but steps[] still has one pending E2E step and no receipt. Can the agent hand off to /he:ship, or must it stay in /he:verify?
Implementation is done but /he:verify has not run yet and work is not committed. The user asks to go straight to /he:ship. What should happen?
For each HE stage, what happens when it fails? I do not want failures to skip forward or get lost.
During /he:verify, thermo finds a missing ownership check and also notices the React pre-push guard is missing. What state updates and loop target are required?

/he:implement changed React/Next TypeScript files and there is no deterministic pre-push guard yet. What must /he:implement add or wire, and what must it record in he-state.json guardrails[] before /he:verify and /he:ship?
/he:implement changed Flutter app code. There is no pre-push quality guard. Which HE stage owns adding or wiring that guard, and what must it record in he-state.json guardrails[] before ship?
/he:ship passed, CI is green, there were no repeated misses, no review gaps, and no pipeline finding to harden. Is /he:learn mandatory?

/he:ship exposed the third occurrence of the same PR evidence problem. There is no script, test, hook, or skill guard yet. What stage should happen, and what stateful handoff should it leave before calling the loop complete?
I finished grill-me. The final plan.md already has accepted vertical slices S1-S6 with acceptance checks. What is the next workflow step?
I finished grill-me and wrote a brief plan.md with product decisions and a verification section, but it has no vertical slices, task waves, or agent-ready issue breakdown. What is next?
The final plan.md already has accepted slices S1-S6, but now I explicitly want separate agent-ready issue cards from those slices. Which workflow step should run?

Another LLM said after grill-me I must run to-issues next. Check that advice and say what should happen next for implementation: plan.md already has vertical slices, task waves, traceability, acceptance checks, and verification.
Grill-me only aligned the direction for a broad cross-package feature. There is no PRD, no accepted implementation brief, and no slices yet. What is the planning route before build?
I need to understand callers and impact, then summarize failing test logs. Is codebase-memory the stage I should run?
We have debugged this exact MCP health check before and there is already a reusable probe script for it. Should the agent reason from scratch again or run the existing deterministic process first?
This is the third time the same markdown policy violation appears. No lint, scanner, hook/test/eval gate exists. What should implementation leave behind?

A project-level AGENTS.md got bloated. Global .agents/AGENTS.md already owns general AGENTS.md hygiene, compactness, token-budget policy, and workflow routing. When fixing the project AGENTS.md, should the agent add a local Budget section, npm run check:agents, package changes, and a new token-budget script, or keep only repo-specific additions?

A project AGENTS.md needs cleanup. The useful project facts are an exact Codebase Memory key, a Codex Cloud setup command, out-of-scope mobile paths, a repo-local ./scripts/aw wrapper, and backend invariants. The global AGENTS.md already covers skills, validation, evidence, token hygiene, and generic best practices. What belongs in the project file?

Exact project-level regression: `<app-repo>/AGENTS.md` was compressed from 232 lines to 32, but added `## Budget`, `<=1000 cl100k_base`, `npm run check:agents`, `scripts/check-agents-token-budget.mjs`, package changes, and a local hook. Keep only repo facts: Codebase Memory key `<repo-memory-key>`, `bash scripts/codex-cloud-setup.sh`, out-of-scope `mobile-app/`, `mobile-apps/`, `docs/MOBILE.md`, repo-local `./scripts/aw`, Appwrite project `<project-id>`, paise, UTC HH:MM, and RBAC roles. Global `.agents/AGENTS.md` owns workflow routing, AGENTS hygiene, token budget policy, and generic best practices. What should the agent keep or reject?
This is a React/Next.js performance cleanup with TS code health concerns before PR.
I am changing a React app dashboard UI. It has hardcoded spacing/colors and duplicated card components. What should the agent check before and after editing?
On a React app, I suspect copy-pasted components and clone groups. It should run React Doctor, but what else must be included for code health before PR?
The UI task is small: add a reusable filter toolbar and tweak button styling. There might be no design system. Can the agent just hardcode the styles?
Set up Sentry and triage a production Sentry issue with observability context.
The checkout UI changed and a user clicks through the flow. What proof is required?
Review this PR harshly for maintainability, wrappers, file bloat, weak tests, and wrong ownership.
I implemented a user-visible feature. Explain the order if targeted tests pass, thermo review finds issues, and then E2E finds a bug. When can no-mistakes start?

This feature touches auth permissions and possible data exposure, and I explicitly asked you to check performance for a slow dashboard query. Where do security-review and performance-rescue fit in the Plan / Implement / Verify flow, and when can no-mistakes start?
I invoked grill-me for a UI flow. We need to decide between two onboarding routes and I cannot judge it from text alone. Where do component/state artifacts fit in the Plan / Implement / Verify flow?
/he:plan has an open Grill Me question, the user has not confirmed alignment, and there are still UI unknowns. Can it say Next ready for /he:implement yes if it writes a good summary?

During /he:plan we need to choose between UI directions. The agent has not reviewed the real app route or an explicit fallback design-system mock in Impeccable Live, has no Lavish poll receipt, and has not saved the selected components. What should it do before implementation?
Build saved filters. I roughly know what I want, just start coding.
While implementing saved filters, also add team-shared filters and permissions.
The feature is implemented and committed. Validate it, push safely, open or update the PR, and follow CI.
gh-fix-ci: Fix failing GitHub Actions on this PR, but Actions cost money. What should the agent do before pushing reruns?
I created a Treehouse worktree and no-mistakes says an explicit refspec git push --dry-run passed. Can we treat that as the local gate, or what must happen first?

This repo is a Flutter app with tracked .git-hooks/pre-push instead of Husky. A new worktree was created for the feature. What hook-readiness check is required before implementation and before no-mistakes?
Use BMAD style and run PRD, DS, QA, then dev using those menu codes.
I need to wire a route into an existing 820-line controller. Can we just connect it and move on?
Change the user status enum and update the API behavior. What else is required before the fix is done?
After editing config docs, how should the final change report be shaped?
